So I want to understand a little bit about kind of your guys' differentiation in the space.

So the biggest thing is we are creating technology for the way audit and advisory works versus a lot of vendors in the space which are expecting audit and advisory to change how they work to fit the tech.
We have practitioners in every department across the company working hand-in-hand with AI experts to actually create purpose-built tech for A&A workers.
Workflows.
I'm to think.
I'm trying to think of a way to phrase this.
So one really important thing to know is that audit and advisory are risk-averse by necessity.
You know, auditors need to find the receipts for everything.
That has made this profession a little more reticent to jump on new teams.
And so one of the things we hear a lot is we're very transparent and forward with security and governance and kind of the stuff that makes them pause.
Like we have our ISO 42001 SOC 2, you know, every certification for cybersecurity and AI governance that exists, which is helping a lot of them move over that hurdle.
Gotcha.

So, for example, with Fieldguide, clients have seen, have been able to cut audit times by 50%, for example, eliminate up to 70% of manual tasks, real-time visibility across audits, and then I think like, Amanda, correct me if I'm wrong, but also like what's a strong benefit is that everything is like in one platform.
Instead of having to manage everything across different filing systems, across different...
Providers, et cetera, or point of view, that's what it's like.
It's the only unified end-to-end AI platform.
So from the first steps where you're requesting data from the client out through reporting.

Wow, yeah.
And I think that that's like, yeah, because when I first started here, there was a talent crisis.
I don't know, Amanda, you would know if like we still have the same talent crisis, but it was like one way to address the talent shortage was, you know, use AI, use fieldguide.
Yeah, something I can chime in on that.
It sounds almost antithetical, but it's very much practitioner-first AI.
So one of the big concerns is AI is replacing people, but we come at it from the ethos of the future is AI plus people.
We just put out something called the AI Maturity Framework just about two months ago that's helping firms go step-by-step through AI autonomy from a practitioner-first lens.

So pointing out the cultural and governance needs around, like, needing to upskill or needing to, you know, convert people from essentially taskmasters into overseeing the way agentic can execute these manual things.
No one got into audit to reconcile Excel files.
And so by taking away that part and just pointing out, like, you still have a job, it just gets to be a cooler job now.
It's bringing a lot back.
We are still in a little bit of a talent crisis.
It's leaning more...

For talent stagnation, there are, for a while, there was just no one taking the CPA exam, and that's bouncing back a little bit, but what we're running into a lot is early and mid-level, or really early and late career people who just don't have these more modern skills.
So they don't know how to write prompts, they don't know how to, you know, oversee an agent's output.
So we're really doing a lot of work to try and reconcile that and remind them that that's still professional judgment, you just have some help getting the output.
